title: Soaring costs fuel retirees’ fears
full_text:
The cost of a comfortable retirement has jumped more than 26 per cent in just five years, fanning fears among older Australians that they will run out of money.
New research reveals it costs close to $80,000 annually for a couple to retire comfortably, up from about $63,000 during the pandemic, amid surging inflation that has driven up spending for seniors by more than $310 a week.
Self-funded retirees are finding the lump sum they retired with years ago has been rapidly eroded by inflation amid high consumer price index increases since 2021.
Pensioners are more protected from rising costs through CPI indexation of their age pension payments.
“Pensioners get CPI adjustments, but self-funded retirees don’t get a pay rise,” Australian Independent Retirees chief advocate Wayne Strandquist said.
“The only way is if they draw down more from their superannuation on a regular basis, and if they’re under cost pressure, they are very much worried about drawing too much out too quickly and running out of money before they run out of time.” Meanwhile, pre-retirees have less confidence their super will be enough in the face of sharply climbing living costs, prompting more to over-estimate how much money they will need. Others plan to work beyond age 70.
The Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia has again increased its forecast for how much a couple now needs to retire. Its latest quarterly data shows that a couple needs an annual income of $78,566 to retire comfortably, while a single person requires $55,923.
An analysis of ASFA’s figures shows costs have accelerated dramatically since 2021, and big increases in bills for electricity, fuel, water charges, council rates and some foods are the key culprits for the latest annual rise.
The cost of a comfortable retirement has climbed 33 per cent in the past 10 years and 65 per cent in the past 20, effectively eroding the purchasing power of retirees’ nest eggs by one-third and two-thirds respectively.
“Inflation is changing how Australians think about their financial futures,” ASFA chief executive Mary Delahunty said.
“When households really feel the pressure of grocery, petrol, energy and other bills that keep climbing, people naturally assume that retirement will cost a fortune.” ASFA says to fund a comfortable retirement singles need a super balance of $630,000 and couples $730,000, but its new polling has found that 42 per cent of people believe they will need more than $1m.
Ms Delahunty said retirement generally costs less than working life. “Retirees pay no tax on superannuation pension income after 60, most own their home outright, work-related costs disappear, and concessions reduce the price of energy, medicines, transport and council rates,” she said.
However, anxiety is intensifying, with separate research by life insurance company TAL finding 36 per cent of pre-retirees aged over 55 expect to work into their 70s, and less than one-third say they have enough money left over for regular saving or investing.
TAL’s general manager of retirement and wealth, Shaun Bransdon, said the cost of living played a key role, but retirement concerns also reflected fewer older Australians owning homes outright.
“That means more people are reaching retirement with significant debt, or ongoing housing costs they need to fund,” he said.
“In our research, 46 per cent of pre-retirees expect their super to run out before the 20-year mark.
“Part of the anxiety comes from the fact that retirees tend to face higher inflation than the general population because they spend more on essentials like healthcare, groceries and electricity.” Nexia Sydney wealth management partner Christine Atencia said that before the pandemic retirees were not talking about inflation, but today more were concerned about the purchasing power of their assets being eroded.
“It makes sense because as inflation goes up, life becomes more expensive and people feel pressure to withdraw more than usual from their assets, like super, portfolios and properties, along with the expectation that their investments need to work harder than before to support the same lifestyle,” she said.
Ms Atencia said she was having more conversations about longevity risk with clients, who were “seeking more frequent reassurance about whether they will have enough assets to support their retirement into the foreseeable future”.
Mr Strandquist said many self-funded retirees he had spoken to had believed the ASFA estimates were less than they should be “because inflation’s moving faster”.
“They are finding they’re spending more than what ASFA is allocating ... living costs have been pushed ahead,” he said.
Mr Strandquist said he had noticed increasing anxiety “and a bit of angst – they are being blamed for a lot of things”.
“Even though self-funded retirees don’t draw an age pension, younger people are quite critical of how somehow retirees have increased the cost of housing,” he said. “Younger people who are priced out of real estate blame the older people for somehow pushing the prices up, but older people have often had properties for 25-30 years and the price isn’t driven by us, it’s driven by lack of supply and inflation.”
